key_insight: DBMol uses structure prediction models to iteratively improve and then chemically validly project molecules toward stronger, target-specific protein-pocket binding.
clinical_relevance: If validated further, this approach could help speed the discovery of more selective small-molecule drug candidates for hard-to-target proteins.
method: Gradient-based optimization improves a starting molecule’s pocket interactions and predicted affinity using a structure prediction model.
A flow-matching projection step converts the optimized molecular graph into discrete, chemically valid molecules.
The framework is evaluated with Boltz-2 affinity proxies and held-out AF3-based metrics to reduce self-confirmation bias.
evidence_signal: The abstract reports that DBMol improves Boltz-2 affinity proxy scores, increases pocket coverage, and remains competitive on held-out AF3-based evaluation while preserving diversity.
watch_item: Predicted gains may not translate to real-world binding or drug-likeness, so experimental validation and careful control of model bias remain essential.
paper_title: DBMol: Design of High-Affinity, Target-Specific Small Molecules through Structure Prediction Models
